measures.context: A reduction of 2.0 or more points on the NPRS represents a clinically meaningful improvement in pain.
measures.source: Farrar et al., 2001; Childs et al., 2005

measures.context: A reduction of 12 or more points on the ODI indicates clinically meaningful improvement in lumbar disability.
An improvement of 2.0 or more points per activity on the PSFS represents a clinically meaningful functional gain.
measures.source: Stratford et al., 1995; Cleland et al., 2006

measures.context: An improvement of 9 or more points on the LEFS indicates clinically meaningful improvement in lower extremity function.
measures.source: Binkley et al., 1999; Stratford et al., 1995

measures.full_name: Disabilities of the Arm, Shoulder and Hand (DASH)
measures.context: A reduction of 10.83 or more points on the DASH represents clinically meaningful improvement in upper extremity function.
measures.source: Beaton et al., 2001; Schmitt & Di Fabio, 2004

measures.context: A reduction of 16 or more points on the QuickDASH indicates clinically meaningful improvement.
A reduction of 7.5 or more points on the NDI represents clinically meaningful improvement in cervical function.
A score of +4 or higher on the GROC scale indicates the patient perceives a meaningful overall improvement.
measures.source: Jaeschke et al., 1989; Schmitt & Di Fabio, 2004

measures.context: An improvement of 50 or more meters on the 6MWT represents a clinically meaningful gain in functional ambulation.
A reduction of 1.4 or more seconds on the TUG indicates clinically meaningful improvement in functional mobility.
measures.source: Flansbjer et al., 2005; Huang et al., 2011

measures.full_name: Knee Injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score (KOOS)
measures.context: An improvement of 10 or more points per KOOS subscale represents clinically meaningful improvement.

measures.full_name: Hip Disability and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score (HOOS)
measures.context: An improvement of 10 or more points per HOOS subscale indicates clinically meaningful improvement.
An improvement of 8 or more points on the FAAM ADL subscale represents clinically meaningful improvement in foot/ankle function.
